items.prompt: Which distinctive scale benefit does Section 12.3.3 emphasize?
choices: Pin training to a lakehouse snapshot without re-copying petabytes
Only printing paper changelogs for every row
Deleting all history after each successful run
items.explain: Section 12.3.3 lists snapshot pinning without petabyte re-copy among distinctive scale gains.

items.prompt: Example 12.18 recommends rebuilding the entire feature table from scratch after every corrupt preprocessing job.
items.explain: Example 12.18 rolls back to the last good snapshot and reruns from a known state.

items.prompt: What storage practice helps manage version history cost at petabyte scale?
choices: Store diffs via delta/snapshot formats, tier cold snapshots, and expire unused history
Keep unlimited full copies of every petabyte table forever
Disable all snapshots to save nothing
Store only the latest corrupt overwrite
items.explain: Section 12.3.4 discusses diffs, tiering cold snapshots, and expiring unused history.

items.prompt: Name one concurrency challenge when evolving schemas on a shared lakehouse table.
items.rubric: Mention coordinated metadata, compatible readers, or deciding when a breaking change needs a new table identity.
items.sample_answer: Coordinating metadata and readers, or deciding when a break needs a new table identity.

items.prompt: General commit hygiene and changelog discipline still belong primarily in Chapter 8, while Chapter 12 focuses on lakehouse-scale overhead and concurrency.
items.explain: Section 12.3.4 explicitly places general versioning discipline in Chapter 8.
